title: Probabilistic graphical models concept

publication.changeSummary: Source-linked AI Foundations draft for Probabilistic graphical models concept; all claims, examples, metrics, code, safety boundaries, and answer oracles require independent human review. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

sources.locator: CS2023 Artificial Intelligence; August 2023; Knowledge Representation and Reasoning plus Reasoning Under Uncertainty; atomic topic: Probabilistic graphical models concept
sources.claim: A Bayesian network is a directed acyclic factorization of a joint distribution in which graph structure encodes conditional independences under the modeled probability assumptions.

canonicalExplanation.summary: A Bayesian network is a directed acyclic factorization of a joint distribution in which graph structure encodes conditional independences under the modeled probability assumptions.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: Rain and sprinkler both point to wet grass, so they become dependent after observing the common effect even if they were marginally independent in the model. Your goal is to construct and reason over a small directed probabilistic model without claiming causal meaning automatically. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: AI problems and agents; Problem and environment characteristics; Conditional probability and Bayes rule; dsa graphs model. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Bounded operational model
sections.body: A Bayesian network is a directed acyclic factorization of a joint distribution in which graph structure encodes conditional independences under the modeled probability assumptions. Any capability or quality claim remains limited to an explicit task, population, conditions, baseline, and recorded evidence.

sections.heading: Failure boundary and non-claim
sections.body: An edge does not by itself establish causation, missing edges assert conditional independence only relative to the graph, latent variables can distort interpretation, and parameters need valid normalized distributions. The learner must reject this overclaim: Every directed edge in a Bayesian network proves that changing the parent will cause the child to change.

includes: Read dependencies and conditional independence from a small Bayesian-network structure.
Construct and reason over a small directed probabilistic model without claiming causal meaning automatically.
excludes: An edge does not by itself establish causation, missing edges assert conditional independence only relative to the graph, latent variables can distort interpretation, and parameters need valid normalized distributions.

outcomes: Construct and reason over a small directed probabilistic model without claiming causal meaning automatically.

examples.title: Probabilistic graphical models concept: bounded direct case
examples.situation: Rain and sprinkler both point to wet grass, so they become dependent after observing the common effect even if they were marginally independent in the model.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Observe the exact data, state, metric, or tool behavior: Rain and sprinkler both point to wet grass, so they become dependent after observing the common effect even if they were marginally independent in the model.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the claim boundary with: Read the graph as a probabilistic factorization and conditional-independence claim; causal interpretation requires additional assumptions and evidence.
examples.result: This example supports only the atomic outcome because Read the graph as a probabilistic factorization and conditional-independence claim; causal interpretation requires additional assumptions and evidence.

examples.title: Probabilistic graphical models concept: stress and transfer case
examples.situation: A learned arrow between advertising and sales is reported as probabilistic dependency, not causal effect, because intervention and confounding evidence are absent.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Name the changed population, input, assumption, or failure: A learned arrow between advertising and sales is reported as probabilistic dependency, not causal effect, because intervention and confounding evidence are absent.

Step 2 — Keep the non-claim visible: An edge does not by itself establish causation, missing edges assert conditional independence only relative to the graph, latent variables can distort interpretation, and parameters need valid normalized distributions.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting overclaim: Every directed edge in a Bayesian network proves that changing the parent will cause the child to change.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Construct and reason over a small directed probabilistic model without claiming causal meaning automatically.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Every directed edge in a Bayesian network proves that changing the parent will cause the child to change.
misconceptions.correction: Read the graph as a probabilistic factorization and conditional-independence claim; causal interpretation requires additional assumptions and evidence.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Identify the first unsupported inference in this belief and the evidence required to narrow it: Every directed edge in a Bayesian network proves that changing the parent will cause the child to change.

analogy.example: an evidence board that distinguishes explicit facts, supported rule chains, missing knowledge, probabilities, and dependency assumptions can introduce Probabilistic graphical models concept only after its canonical technical definition and evidence boundary are stated.
usefulFor: symbolic representation, inference, Bayesian updating, and graphical dependencies
limitations: encoded facts can be incomplete or inconsistent and probabilistic edges do not automatically establish causation

recap.summary: You should now be able to construct and reason over a small directed probabilistic model without claiming causal meaning automatically. Keep this boundary in view: An edge does not by itself establish causation, missing edges assert conditional independence only relative to the graph, latent variables can distort interpretation, and parameters need valid normalized distributions.
